text:
The cathodic transfer coefficient α c is defined in the IUPAC Green Book edition 3 as (RT/nF)(dlnkc/dE) where k c is the rate constant for the electroreduction reaction and E is the electrode potential. It can also be expressed as (RT/nF)(dln|jc|/dE) if the expression for the cathodic current density jc is modified to take into account the concentration gradient in the solution in the electrode environment; an analogous definition holds for the anodic transfer coefficient α a.

A persistent source of confusion and error in the scientific literature is the inapprop-riate extension of a definition that is rigorously applicable only to a single elementary act consisting of the simultaneous transfer of n electrons. Under the normal conditions pertinent to any redox process, the fundamental Marcus theory dictates that the transfer of n electrons should not occur in several steps, but rather in single electron transfers throughout, as the required electronic reorganization for the transfer of additional electrons between the reactants and products at opposite extremes of the activation energy volcano is prohibitively high. The definition of the electron transfer number (often denoted as n) has been carried over for convenience into the context of a multistage (multiprobe) mechanism, as well as being “derived” from experiment. Most such “deriving” or “evaluation” procedures amount to no more than the calculation of the value of (RT/F)(d ln|j|/dE) equality to αn, with the evaluation of n thereafter being undertaken by using the default value of α taken to be about 0.5. An important source of confusion arises from the common practice of again identifying n with the total number of electrons moved during the catalytic reaction as a whole, or specifically with the number of electrons considered to be the amount transferred during the purported rate-determining step(s).

Based on the history of the transfer coefficient and a discussion of the conditions for concerted multi-electron transfer we propose that the transfer coefficient should no longer be defined within the context of any particular mechanism. Instead we suggest that the cathodic and anodic transfer coefficients be defined as $\alpha_c = -(RT/F)(d\ln|j_c|/dE)$ and $\alpha_a = (RT/F)(d\ln j_a/dE)$. These quantities are based solely on the experimental slopes that are directly measurable and are therefore free from any mechanistic assumptions.
